content.text: Agent loops can be bounded by two fundamentally different budget types:
How many reasoning steps the agent can take
When latency matters more than cost; simple per-iteration bound is predictable
Input tokens + output tokens (or cost in dollars)
The monetary cost and context window growth of the run
When cost control is required; more precise than iteration counting

Iteration budgets are simpler to implement but imprecise: one iteration can consume 100 tokens (short Thought, small tool result) or 5,000 tokens (long Thought, large tool result). Token budgets are more accurate but require reading
from every API call. Use both in production: the iteration budget as a backstop, the token budget as the primary control.
The Anthropic API returns token usage in every response:
. Cumulate these across iterations to track total run cost.
dictionary encodes per-model costs per million tokens. Update it when pricing changes — the agent loop code does not need to change. The
: it accounts for the cost difference between input and output tokens (output tokens are typically 4–5x more expensive than input tokens per token).

Different task types have different expected costs. A single lookup should cost under $0.001. A multi-step research task may legitimately cost $0.02–0.05. Using a single global budget for all task types either over-restricts cheap tasks or under-restricts expensive ones. The solution is

from enum import Enum

class TaskTier(Enum):
    LOOKUP = "lookup"
    RESEARCH = "research"
    CODE = "code"
    SUMMARISE = "summarise"
    ANALYSIS = "analysis"

TIER_BUDGETS = {
    TaskTier.LOOKUP:    TokenBudget("claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_input_tokens=10_000,  max_cost_usd=0.005),
    TaskTier.RESEARCH:  TokenBudget("claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_input_tokens=80_000,  max_cost_usd=0.05),
    TaskTier.CODE:      TokenBudget("claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_input_tokens=50_000,  max_cost_usd=0.03),
    TaskTier.SUMMARISE: TokenBudget("claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_input_tokens=40_000,  max_cost_usd=0.02),
    TaskTier.ANALYSIS:  TokenBudget("claude-haiku-4-5-20251001", max_input_tokens=150_000, max_cost_usd=0.10),
}

def get_budget(tier: TaskTier) -> TokenBudget:
    """Return a fresh budget instance for the given task tier."""
    template = TIER_BUDGETS[tier]
    return TokenBudget(
        model=template.model,
        max_input_tokens=template.max_input_tokens,
        max_cost_usd=template.max_cost_usd,
    )

# Usage: select the tier based on the incoming request type
# budget = get_budget(TaskTier.RESEARCH)
# ... run agent loop with budget tracking ...
# print(f"Final: {budget.summary()}")
The tier is selected by the host code based on the task's characteristics, not by the model. For a web API, the tier can be derived from the route (
-> RESEARCH) or from a classifier that runs before the agent loop. The budget object is created fresh for each run — it must not be shared across runs.

An agent that knows it has limited budget will prioritise its actions differently. An agent that does not know its budget may spend freely on low-value calls before realising it has no budget left for the critical final step. The
pattern passes the remaining token count to the agent as a system prompt update before each API call.

The system prompt is rebuilt before every API call with the current remaining token count. At 50% remaining, a neutral note encourages prioritisation. At 25% remaining, an urgent warning tells the agent to wrap up. This gives the agent
to adjust its behavior before the hard limit. Without the injections, the agent only finds out about the limit when it is cut off — too late to produce a useful partial answer.
A fixed iteration cap is a blunt instrument. A research task that finds the answer in 3 iterations is forced to stop at 3, even though 3 more iterations might improve the answer. An
adjusts the cap based on observed cost per iteration, allowing more iterations when they are cheap and fewer when they are expensive.
to the host code. The host code can use this to set the
of the next API call proportionally (e.g., allocate
to output). The minimum iterations guard (
) ensures the agent is never cut off after just 1-2 iterations even if the first iteration was expensive.
When an orchestrator spawns sub-agents, the
total budget must be allocated across all agents

, not just the orchestrator. A common mistake is giving each sub-agent an independent budget equal to the task total: 5 sub-agents each with a $0.05 budget means up to $0.25 in total cost — 5x the intended spend.
All agents draw from a single shared budget object; first to exhaust stops
Correct total cost; one expensive sub-agent can starve others
Total budget split equally among N agents before spawning
Predictable per-agent cost; hard to adjust if one agent finishes early
Orchestrator holds the pool; sub-agents request budget slices; unused tokens returned

import anthropic
import json
from threading import Lock

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

class SharedTokenPool:
    """
    Thread-safe token pool shared across parallel sub-agents.
    Each sub-agent draws tokens and returns any unused allocation.
    """
    def __init__(self, total_tokens: int):
        self._remaining = total_tokens
        self._lock = Lock()
        self._spent = 0

    def request(self, tokens_requested: int) -> int:
        """Request an allocation. Returns actual tokens granted (may be less)."""
        with self._lock:
            granted = min(tokens_requested, self._remaining)
            self._remaining -= granted
            return granted

    def record_usage(self, tokens_used: int):
        """Record actual usage after an API call."""
        with self._lock:
            self._spent += tokens_used

    def return_unused(self, tokens_unused: int):
        """Return unused allocation back to the pool."""
        with self._lock:
            self._remaining += tokens_unused

    @property
    def is_empty(self) -> bool:
        with self._lock:
            return self._remaining <= 0

    def summary(self) -> dict:
        with self._lock:
            return {"spent": self._spent, "remaining": self._remaining}

def run_sub_agent(pool: SharedTokenPool, task: str, allocation: int) -> dict:
    """
    Sub-agent that draws from the shared pool.
    Returns unused tokens when done.
    """
    granted = pool.request(allocation)
    if granted == 0:
        return {"status": "budget_denied", "task": task}

    tokens_used = 0
    # Simulate 2 iterations of ~1,500 tokens each
    for _ in range(2):
        if tokens_used + 1500 > granted:
            break
        # In a real agent: resp = client.messages.create(...)
        # tokens_used += resp.usage.input_tokens
        tokens_used += 1500  # stub

    pool.record_usage(tokens_used)
    unused = granted - tokens_used
    if unused > 0:
        pool.return_unused(unused)

    return {"status": "complete", "task": task, "tokens_used": tokens_used}

# Orchestrator: total budget of 15,000 tokens shared across 3 sub-agents
pool = SharedTokenPool(total_tokens=15_000)

from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor
tasks = ["Summarise report A", "Summarise report B", "Summarise report C"]
allocation_per_agent = 6_000  # each requests 6,000 but pool only has 15,000

with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=3) as executor:
    futures = [executor.submit(run_sub_agent, pool, task, allocation_per_agent)
               for task in tasks]
    results = [f.result() for f in futures]

for r in results:
    print(r)
print(pool.summary())
# Each agent uses 3,000 tokens (2 x 1,500); pool spent = 9,000, remaining = 6,000
to make all operations thread-safe. The
method grants tokens on a first-come-first-served basis. When a sub-agent finishes early,
makes those tokens available to other agents still running. The orchestrator should check
before spawning additional sub-agents — if the pool is empty, later sub-agents will be denied budget and return a
Use both iteration and token budgets together — the iteration cap is the backstop, the token budget is the primary control.

Track cumulative input and output tokens separately (output tokens cost more per token). Assign per-task budget tiers based on expected task complexity. Inject remaining-budget warnings at 50% and 25% thresholds so the agent can wrap up gracefully. Include the budget summary in every non-complete exit status for observability.
